categories.title: Composite Scores & Signals
categories.description: Overall rollup metrics that summarize multiple underlying factors into a single score.

entries.name: Composite Score
entries.what: A single 0-100 score that rolls up the four pillar scores (Business Quality, Operational Health, Capital Allocation, Growth) into one overall quality metric for the company.
entries.how: Weighted average of the pillar scores, typically with Business Quality and Operational Health weighted slightly higher than Growth and Capital Allocation. A higher score means better overall fundamentals.
entries.why: Gives you a quick one-number read on whether a company is high-quality across multiple dimensions, rather than forcing you to mentally average the pillars yourself. Companies scoring above 70 are generally solid; below 50 have meaningful weaknesses somewhere.
entries.watch_for: A high composite doesn't mean 'buy' — it means 'well-run.' A well-run company can still be overvalued. Also, a composite in the middle (50-70) can hide a great score on one pillar offset by a terrible score on another. Always check the pillar breakdown.

entries.name: Data Completeness
entries.what: The percentage of expected data fields that were successfully populated from the data providers (Polygon, Finnhub, FMP, etc.) during evaluation.
entries.how: Calculated as (fields actually populated / fields expected for a full evaluation) × 100. A company with 95% completeness has nearly all the data the evaluator expects; one with 60% has meaningful gaps.
entries.why: Tells you how much trust to place in the other metrics. A composite score of 72 means something different if it was calculated from 95% of the data vs 55% of the data — the latter might be missing critical inputs that would have changed the result.
entries.watch_for: Below 75% completeness, take the pillar scores and valuations with substantial skepticism. Newly-IPO'd companies, foreign ADRs, and small caps with sparse reporting often have lower completeness, which limits what the evaluator can say confidently.

entries.name: Breakout Score
entries.what: A technical score estimating the probability that the stock is setting up for a meaningful upward price move based on recent price action, volume, and momentum indicators.
entries.how: Combines inputs like distance above moving averages, volume trend, RSI momentum, and 52-week range position into a 0-100 score. Higher scores suggest stronger technical setup.
entries.why: Complements the fundamental quality analysis with a 'when to buy' signal. A great company with a low breakout score might be worth waiting on; a lower-quality company with a high breakout score might be a short-term trade but not a long-term hold.
entries.watch_for: Technical scores are short-term signals that can flip quickly with market conditions. Don't use breakout score alone — combine it with the fundamental composite for a complete picture.

entries.name: LLM Recommendation
entries.what: A BUY/HOLD/SELL recommendation generated by a language model that synthesizes the fundamental, valuation, and technical data into a plain-English investment stance.
entries.how: An LLM is given the full evaluation output and asked to classify the opportunity, returning a recommendation plus a conviction score (0-100%) representing how certain it is about the call.
entries.why: Provides a narrative-style synthesis that's easier to reason about than looking at 20 separate metrics. Useful as a sanity check against your own interpretation.
entries.watch_for: LLMs can hallucinate or anchor on recent training data. Treat the recommendation as one opinion among many, not gospel. High conviction (>75%) with consistent signals across other sections is more trustworthy than high conviction based on one or two metrics.

categories.title: Financial Distress & Health
categories.description: Metrics that assess the company's ability to survive and avoid bankruptcy.

entries.name: Altman Z-Score
entries.what: A composite score that estimates the probability a company will go bankrupt within 2 years. Developed by Edward Altman in 1968 and still one of the most reliable distress predictors available.
entries.how: Z = 1.2×(Working Capital/Total Assets) + 1.4×(Retained Earnings/Total Assets) + 3.3×(EBIT/Total Assets) + 0.6×(Market Cap/Total Liabilities) + 1.0×(Sales/Total Assets). Zones: Safe (Z ≥ 2.99), Watch (1.81-2.99), Distress (Z < 1.81).
entries.why: Bankruptcy destroys shareholder value completely — you go to zero. Before investing in a company, knowing whether it's likely to survive is table stakes. The Z-score bundles five different financial health signals into one actionable zone classification.
entries.watch_for: The formula was designed for manufacturing companies and can produce misleading results for financial firms, REITs, and asset-light tech companies. Very profitable companies with minimal physical assets can score lower than they should. Use it as a directional signal, not an absolute verdict.

entries.name: Current Ratio
entries.what: A liquidity ratio that measures whether a company has enough short-term assets to pay its short-term liabilities.
entries.how: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities. A ratio of 1.0 means assets exactly cover liabilities; above 1.5 is generally considered healthy; below 1.0 is a red flag.
entries.why: Companies go bankrupt when they can't pay their bills, even if they're theoretically profitable. The current ratio is the most basic test of whether a company can meet its obligations over the next 12 months.
entries.watch_for: Very high current ratios (above 3) can indicate inefficient capital use — the company is hoarding cash or inventory instead of deploying it productively. Very low ratios in stable, cash-generative businesses (like utilities or subscription software) may be fine because revenue is predictable.

entries.name: Debt-to-EBITDA Ratio
entries.what: A leverage ratio measuring how many years of current earnings it would take to pay off the company's debt.
entries.how: Debt/EBITDA = Total Debt / EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization). Ratios below 3x are generally considered manageable; above 5x is high leverage.
entries.why: Debt amplifies returns in good times and amplifies losses in bad times. This ratio tells you how stressed the company would be if earnings declined — a 6x leveraged company loses far more room to maneuver than a 2x leveraged one.
entries.watch_for: Industries with predictable cash flows (utilities, telecom) can safely carry higher leverage. Cyclical industries (autos, semiconductors) need lower leverage because their EBITDA can swing dramatically. Always compare to industry norms.

entries.name: Interest Coverage Ratio
entries.what: A measure of how easily a company can pay the interest on its outstanding debt using current operating income.
entries.how: Interest Coverage = Operating Income / Interest Expense. A ratio of 3x or higher is healthy; below 1.5x is concerning. Companies with essentially no debt show 'N/A — no debt' because there's nothing to cover.
entries.why: Missing interest payments triggers technical default even for profitable companies. Interest coverage tells you how much cushion exists between what the company earns and what it owes — critical for understanding financial fragility.
entries.watch_for: A sudden drop in interest coverage year-over-year is a stronger warning signal than the absolute number. A company going from 8x to 4x coverage is deteriorating fast even though 4x is still technically 'healthy.'

categories.title: Quality Signals
categories.description: Multi-factor screens that combine several individual checks into an overall quality assessment.

entries.name: Piotroski F-Score
entries.what: A 9-point accounting-based quality score developed by Joseph Piotroski (2000) that tests whether a company's fundamentals are actually improving year-over-year. Each passing check scores 1 point for a total of 0-9.
entries.how: Nine checks across profitability (4), leverage/liquidity (3), and operating efficiency (2). Scores of 8-9 are 'Strong,' 5-7 are 'Average,' and 0-4 are 'Weak.'
entries.why: Piotroski's original research showed that value stocks (low P/B) with F-scores of 8-9 significantly outperformed value stocks with F-scores of 0-2 — over a 20-year backtest. The F-score doesn't pick winners, but it filters out the traps among apparently-cheap companies.
entries.watch_for: The F-score is designed for established companies with multi-year financials. Early-stage growth companies often fail checks like 'long-term debt decreasing' because they're intentionally borrowing to expand. Use it on mature businesses.

entries.name: Insider Activity Score
entries.what: A 0-100 score that quantifies how insiders (officers, directors, major holders) are trading the company's stock over a recent window.
entries.how: Considers buy vs sell volume, number of transactions, dollar values, and insider role. Heavy insider buying pushes the score up; concentrated selling pushes it down. Net direction is either 'buying' or 'selling.'
entries.why: Insiders know their companies better than anyone. Heavy insider buying — especially from multiple insiders at similar prices — is one of the strongest positive signals in equity research. Heavy selling is more ambiguous (insiders sell for many reasons) but can still warn of insider concerns.
entries.watch_for: Insider buying is usually more informative than selling — people buy for one reason (they think it's going up), but sell for many reasons (tax planning, diversification, liquidity, scheduled 10b5-1 sales). Don't read too much into routine sales by long-tenured executives.

categories.title: Valuation Models
categories.description: Different methods of estimating what a company is actually worth based on its fundamentals, cash flows, and peers.

entries.name: DCF Intrinsic Value
entries.what: The theoretical per-share value of a company calculated by discounting its projected future free cash flows back to today at the company's cost of capital.
entries.how: Project free cash flow for 5-10 years, discount each year at WACC, add a terminal value representing all cash flows beyond the projection period, sum them up, divide by share count.
entries.why: DCF is the most theoretically pure valuation method — it values a company based on what it will actually deliver to shareholders in cash, not what the market happens to pay today. Warren Buffett famously considers DCF the 'right way' to value a business.
entries.watch_for: DCF is extremely sensitive to assumptions. Small changes to WACC (e.g., 8% → 9%) or terminal growth rate (e.g., 2.5% → 3%) can shift fair value by 20-30%. Use DCF as a directional check, not a precise price target. Always look at the assumptions before trusting the output.

entries.name: WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)
entries.what: The blended cost of the company's debt and equity financing, weighted by how much of each it uses. Essentially, the minimum return the company must earn to satisfy both lenders and shareholders.
entries.how: WACC = (E/V × Re) + (D/V × Rd × (1-Tax Rate)), where E is equity value, D is debt value, V is total capitalization, Re is cost of equity (via CAPM), Rd is cost of debt.
entries.why: WACC is the discount rate used in DCF and EVA calculations. It represents the 'hurdle rate' — if the company's ROIC is above WACC, it creates value; if ROIC is below WACC, it destroys value. It's the floor a company must beat to justify its own existence.
entries.watch_for: WACC calculations involve CAPM which assumes markets are efficient and betas are meaningful — both assumptions break down for small caps, recent IPOs, and illiquid stocks. Treat WACC estimates as approximate (±1-2 percentage points).

entries.name: Terminal Growth Rate
entries.what: The assumed long-run growth rate used in DCF to value all cash flows beyond the explicit projection period (usually years 6+). Represents growth into perpetuity.
entries.how: Typically set between 2-3% for mature companies (reflecting long-run GDP or inflation). Very rarely set higher than 4% because no company can grow faster than the economy forever.
entries.why: The terminal value usually represents 60-80% of a DCF's total fair value — meaning your terminal growth assumption drives most of the answer. Getting it wrong by 0.5% can swing fair value by 15%.
entries.watch_for: Be skeptical of DCF models using terminal growth above 3%. Most analysts use 2.5% as a default, which is reasonable for the majority of mature businesses. Higher values imply the company will grow faster than the economy forever — which is mathematically impossible.

entries.name: EVA (Economic Value Added)
entries.what: A measure of how much economic profit a company generates above and beyond its cost of capital. Positive EVA means the company is creating value; negative EVA means it's destroying value.
entries.how: EVA = NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax) − (Invested Capital × WACC). Can also be expressed as (ROIC − WACC) × Invested Capital.
entries.why: EVA answers the fundamental question: 'Is this company earning more than the cost of the capital it uses?' A company with high revenue and profit can still destroy value if its ROIC is below WACC. EVA cuts through accounting noise to reveal whether the business is genuinely creating wealth.
entries.watch_for: EVA can be manipulated by how you define 'invested capital' (do you include goodwill? R&D capitalization? operating leases?). Make sure the methodology is consistent when comparing companies.

entries.name: ROIC vs WACC Spread
entries.what: The difference between what a company earns on its invested capital (ROIC) and what that capital costs (WACC). A positive spread means the company is creating economic value; a negative spread means it's destroying value.
entries.how: Spread = ROIC − WACC, measured in percentage points. A spread of +10 pts means the company earns 10% more on its capital than the capital costs, creating real economic profit.
entries.why: This is the single most important measure of whether a company is actually a good business. A company can grow revenue for years while destroying value if its spread is negative. A company with a positive spread compounds value for shareholders over time.
entries.watch_for: Sustained spreads above 10 pts are rare and usually indicate a strong competitive moat. Spreads that were wide and are narrowing may signal competitive pressure. Spreads that are consistently negative are a red flag regardless of growth rate.

entries.name: Peer Comparison Fair Value
entries.what: An estimated fair value derived by applying the average valuation multiples (P/E, P/S, EV/EBITDA) of comparable peer companies to this company's fundamentals.
entries.how: Select 5-10 comparable companies in the same industry, calculate their average P/E, P/S, and EV/EBITDA multiples, apply those averages to this company's earnings, sales, and EBITDA, average the results.
entries.why: Relative valuation is how most of the market actually prices stocks day-to-day. If this company trades at a 30% discount to its peers on identical metrics, that's either an opportunity (it's undervalued) or a warning (the market sees something you don't).
entries.watch_for: Garbage in, garbage out. If the peer group is poorly chosen (wrong industry, wrong size, wrong profitability profile), the output is meaningless. Also, if the entire peer group is overvalued, this method just returns 'equally overvalued as peers' — which isn't actionable.

entries.name: EPV Trailing (1 year)
entries.what: Earnings Power Value calculated using the most recent year's operating income as the earning power base. Represents what the company is worth if its current earnings continue indefinitely at a zero growth rate.
entries.how: EPV = (Most Recent EBIT × (1 - Tax Rate)) / WACC. The formula assumes the company's current operating income will continue forever with no growth.
entries.why: Trailing EPV gives you a 'no-growth base case' valuation — what the company is worth if nothing changes. If the stock trades below trailing EPV, you're essentially getting any future growth for free.
entries.watch_for: Most recent year EBIT can be unusually high (one-time gain, favorable tax year, non-recurring boost) or unusually low (restructuring charge, writedown). Always compare trailing EPV to normalized EPV to see if the recent year is an outlier.

entries.name: EPV Normalized (5 year)
entries.what: Earnings Power Value calculated using a 5-year average of operating income instead of just the most recent year. Smooths out cyclical and non-recurring effects to estimate sustainable earning power.
entries.how: EPV = (Average 5y EBIT × (1 - Tax Rate)) / WACC. Same formula as trailing EPV but uses the normalized earning power base.
entries.why: Normalized EPV is more conservative and usually more meaningful than trailing EPV because it smooths out cyclical swings, one-time gains, and accounting noise. Greenwald (in 'Value Investing') recommends normalized EPV as the baseline for valuation.
entries.watch_for: A 5-year average may not capture a genuine improvement in earning power if the company has fundamentally changed in the last year or two. Look at the EBIT history directly — if earnings have stair-stepped higher and stayed there, trailing may be more relevant than normalized.

entries.name: Growth Premium
entries.what: The difference between the current stock price and the EPV-implied fair value, expressed as a percentage. Tells you how much of the stock's current price is justified by growth assumptions vs current earning power alone.
entries.how: Growth Premium = ((Current Price - EPV) / EPV) × 100. A stock trading at 2× its EPV has a 100% growth premium; a stock trading below EPV has a negative growth premium (a discount).
entries.why: This decomposes the current stock price into two parts: 'value of current earnings' and 'value of expected future growth.' A stock with a 300% growth premium is essentially saying 'most of this price is based on growth that hasn't happened yet.' Understanding that ratio helps you see what you're actually paying for.
entries.watch_for: High growth premiums (200%+) aren't inherently bad — fast-growing quality companies often trade there. But you need to believe the growth story. Deep discounts (-20% or more) can be opportunities or value traps; check why the market is pricing it below EPV.

entries.name: Emergence Signal
entries.what: A classification of the company's recent earning power trajectory based on the shape of its 5-year operating income history. Distinguishes between steady compounders, genuine turnarounds, and possible one-time spikes.
entries.how: Compares trailing (1y) to normalized (5y) EPV ratio, analyzes year-over-year EBIT growth patterns, and checks for post-trough recovery patterns. Produces labels like EMERGING, EXPANDING, GROWING, RECOVERING, STABLE, DECLINING, POSSIBLE_ONE_TIME, or INSUFFICIENT_DATA.
entries.why: Two companies can have identical EPV numbers but very different stories. One might have steadily grown earnings from $100M to $500M (GROWING). Another might have had a one-time $400M gain this year (POSSIBLE_ONE_TIME). The emergence signal tells you which story the data actually supports before you trust the valuation.
entries.watch_for: POSSIBLE_ONE_TIME is the most important signal to heed. It means the recent year's earnings are dramatically above the historical average and may not be sustainable — verify with management commentary or footnotes before treating trailing EPV as real.

categories.title: Pillar Metrics
categories.description: The individual financial metrics that make up the four quality pillars: Business Quality, Operational Health, Capital Allocation, and Growth.

entries.name: Gross Margin
entries.what: The percentage of revenue left after subtracting the direct cost of producing the goods or services sold (cost of goods sold).
entries.how: Gross Margin = (Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold) / Revenue × 100. Software and branded consumer goods often exceed 70%; retail is typically 20-35%; grocery can be below 15%.
entries.why: Gross margin is the purest measure of pricing power and product economics. A company with 70% gross margins has huge flexibility to invest in growth, weather downturns, or return capital. A company with 15% gross margins has razor-thin cushion for anything.
entries.watch_for: Gross margin is meaningless across industries — comparing a software company to an airline is absurd. Only compare gross margin to peers in the same industry, or to the same company's own history. Sudden declines are a warning sign of pricing pressure or rising input costs.

entries.name: Operating Margin
entries.what: The percentage of revenue left after subtracting all operating expenses (COGS plus SG&A plus R&D), but before interest and taxes.
entries.how: Operating Margin = Operating Income / Revenue × 100. Represents how efficient the company is at converting sales into operational profit.
entries.why: Operating margin shows overall operational efficiency, not just unit economics. A company might have great gross margins but spend too much on overhead — that shows up in a weaker operating margin. It's the best single measure of how well the business runs.
entries.watch_for: Operating margins expand and contract with scale. Growing companies often have lower operating margins because they're investing in future capacity. Mature companies with declining operating margins are a warning sign — either pricing power is eroding or costs are rising faster than revenue.

entries.name: Net Margin
entries.what: The percentage of revenue that becomes actual profit after ALL expenses, including interest, taxes, and one-time items.
entries.how: Net Margin = Net Income / Revenue × 100. This is the 'bottom line' profitability ratio that flows through to shareholders.
entries.why: Net margin is what actually matters to shareholders — it's the profit that can be distributed as dividends, used for buybacks, or reinvested. Comparing net margin to operating margin reveals how much is lost to taxes, interest, and non-operating items.
entries.watch_for: Net income is the most manipulable line on the income statement because it includes discretionary items (asset writedowns, tax strategies, one-time gains). A company can smooth earnings or engineer net income short-term. Always sanity-check against free cash flow.

entries.name: Free Cash Flow Yield
entries.what: The company's annual free cash flow expressed as a percentage of its market capitalization. Think of it as the 'earnings yield' for cash flow rather than accounting earnings.
entries.how: FCF Yield = Free Cash Flow / Market Cap × 100. Inverse of the P/FCF multiple. A 5% FCF yield means you're getting $5 of cash flow for every $100 of market cap invested.
entries.why: FCF yield is the cleanest measure of how much actual cash the company generates relative to what you're paying for it. Unlike earnings, FCF is harder to manipulate. A high FCF yield on a quality business is one of the most reliable value signals in equity investing.
entries.watch_for: FCF can be lumpy year-to-year due to working capital swings, one-time capex, or acquisitions. Use a 3-5 year average for a more reliable read. Very high yields (>10%) on stable businesses often indicate the market sees risk you don't.

entries.name: ROIC (Return on Invested Capital)
entries.what: The rate of return the company earns on all the capital that has been invested in the business — both equity and debt.
entries.how: ROIC = NOPAT / Invested Capital. NOPAT is Net Operating Profit After Tax. Invested Capital is equity plus debt minus excess cash.
entries.why: ROIC is the single most important measure of a business's quality. A company that consistently earns 20% ROIC is compounding value 10x faster than one earning 5%. Warren Buffett, Terry Smith, and most quality-focused investors consider high sustained ROIC the defining trait of a great business.
entries.watch_for: ROIC can be inflated by heavy share buybacks or by excluding goodwill from invested capital. Check whether ROIC is 'with goodwill' or 'ex-goodwill' and be consistent. Also look at 5-year ROIC trends — a high ROIC today but declining steadily is a warning.

entries.name: Revenue CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate)
entries.what: The average annual rate at which revenue has grown over a given period (typically 3 or 5 years), expressed as a compounded percentage.
entries.how: CAGR = ((Final Revenue / Initial Revenue)^(1/years)) − 1. A 3y CAGR of 15% means revenue has been growing 15% per year on average for three years.
entries.why: Revenue CAGR is the starting point for any growth analysis. Sustained double-digit revenue growth is rare and valuable. Consistent 15%+ growth over 5 years in a quality business is often worth a premium multiple.
entries.watch_for: CAGR can hide volatility. A 5-year 10% CAGR could be smooth growth or could be four years of zero growth and one year of 60% growth. Always look at year-by-year growth to see the pattern, not just the compound average.

entries.name: Free Cash Flow Growth
entries.what: The year-over-year percentage change in free cash flow, measuring how fast the company's cash-generating ability is expanding.
entries.how: FCF Growth = (Current FCF − Prior FCF) / Prior FCF × 100. Can be measured over any period but typically year-over-year or averaged over 3-5 years.
entries.why: FCF growth is what actually increases intrinsic value. Revenue can grow without FCF growing (if margins compress or working capital balloons). FCF growth means the business is fundamentally getting more valuable year over year.
entries.watch_for: FCF is lumpy. One-time capex projects, acquisitions, or working capital unwinds can produce huge single-year swings that don't reflect the underlying trend. Average over multiple years or look at FCF trends in conjunction with revenue trends.

entries.name: EPS Growth (Year-over-Year)
entries.what: The percentage change in earnings per share from the same period one year ago. Accounts for both profit growth and share count changes.
entries.how: EPS Growth = (Current EPS − Prior EPS) / Prior EPS × 100. EPS itself is Net Income / Diluted Shares Outstanding.
entries.why: EPS growth is the most-watched growth metric because it directly measures per-share value creation. A company growing earnings 15% while buying back 3% of shares is delivering roughly 18% EPS growth to existing holders.
entries.watch_for: EPS can be engineered through buybacks without underlying business improvement. A company with flat net income but declining share count can show 'EPS growth' that's really financial engineering. Cross-check EPS growth against net income growth and FCF growth.

entries.name: Share Count Trend
entries.what: The year-over-year percentage change in shares outstanding, showing whether the company is buying back stock (decreasing shares) or issuing new stock (increasing shares).
entries.how: Share Trend = (Current Shares − Prior Year Shares) / Prior Year Shares × 100. Negative values indicate buybacks; positive values indicate dilution.
entries.why: Share count changes are a direct form of capital return (or capital extraction). A 3% annual reduction in shares compounded over 10 years reduces total shares by ~26%, a huge gift to remaining shareholders. Dilution of 5%+ per year erodes per-share value even if the business grows.
entries.watch_for: Buybacks aren't inherently good — if done at expensive valuations they destroy value. Dilution isn't inherently bad — if used to fund high-return investments it creates value. Look at WHY the share count is changing, not just whether it is.

entries.name: Payout Ratio
entries.what: The percentage of earnings paid out to shareholders as dividends. The rest is reinvested in the business or used for buybacks.
entries.how: Payout Ratio = Dividends per Share / EPS × 100. A 40% payout ratio means the company returns 40 cents of every earned dollar as dividends and retains 60 cents.
entries.why: Payout ratio signals the company's capital allocation philosophy. Low payouts (<30%) suggest the company sees reinvestment opportunities; high payouts (>60%) suggest a mature business with limited growth options returning cash to shareholders.
entries.watch_for: Payout ratios above 100% mean the company is paying more in dividends than it earns — unsustainable unless it's a temporary effect of a bad year. REITs and utilities routinely have payout ratios of 70-90% because of their structure; compare to industry norms.

categories.title: Technical Entry & Price Targets
categories.description: Signals and indicators for deciding when to enter a position and where to set stops and targets.

entries.name: Technical Signal (WAIT/BUY/STRONG BUY)
entries.what: An aggregated technical signal that synthesizes trend, momentum, and range position into a single entry recommendation.
entries.how: Combines trend direction, RSI levels, distance from moving averages, and 52-week range position into a rule-based score, which maps to WAIT (60 or below), BUY (60-80), or STRONG BUY (above 80).
entries.why: Even the best fundamental company can be a bad buy today if the technicals are unfavorable. The technical signal tells you whether today is a reasonable entry point or whether waiting for better prices makes more sense.
entries.watch_for: Technical signals are short-term (days to weeks) and can flip quickly. Don't confuse a STRONG BUY technical signal with a STRONG BUY fundamental thesis — they measure different things on different timeframes.

entries.name: Conviction
entries.what: A 0-100% score representing how confident the technical model is in its signal. Higher conviction means more indicators are aligned in the same direction.
entries.how: Calculated from the number of independent technical indicators agreeing with the primary signal. A WAIT signal with 90% conviction has many indicators pointing to wait; a WAIT with 55% conviction is more of a coin flip.
entries.why: Knowing HOW confident a signal is matters as much as the signal itself. Acting on a 55% conviction BUY is gambling; acting on a 90% conviction BUY after fundamental due diligence is a decision.
entries.watch_for: High conviction isn't always correct — it just means the indicators agree. All indicators can be wrong simultaneously, especially at inflection points or during regime changes.

entries.name: RSI (Relative Strength Index)
entries.what: A momentum oscillator that measures the speed and magnitude of recent price changes to identify overbought or oversold conditions.
entries.how: RSI = 100 − (100 / (1 + RS)), where RS = Average Gain / Average Loss over 14 periods. Values above 70 are considered overbought; below 30 are oversold; 30-70 is neutral.
entries.why: RSI helps identify when a stock's recent move is extreme relative to its own history. A great company with RSI at 85 is likely ripe for a short-term pullback; the same company at RSI 25 may be a short-term opportunity.
entries.watch_for: Strong uptrends can keep RSI above 70 for months. RSI is best used as a contextual indicator, not a trigger by itself. 'Overbought' doesn't mean 'will go down' — it means 'has moved fast recently.'

entries.name: SMA 50 (50-Day Simple Moving Average)
entries.what: The average closing price over the last 50 trading days, forming a line that smooths out short-term volatility and shows the medium-term trend.
entries.how: SMA 50 = Sum of last 50 closing prices / 50. Recalculated daily as the window rolls forward.
entries.why: The 50-day SMA is one of the most-watched technical levels in equity markets. Price above the 50-day is generally considered bullish; crosses above or below often trigger algorithmic trading activity. It's a widely-followed line in the sand.
entries.watch_for: SMAs lag price — by definition they show where the price has been, not where it's going. Breaks above or below the 50-day can be early signals OR false moves that reverse within days.

entries.name: SMA 200 (200-Day Simple Moving Average)
entries.what: The average closing price over the last 200 trading days, representing the long-term trend.
entries.how: SMA 200 = Sum of last 200 closing prices / 200. Recalculated daily.
entries.why: The 200-day SMA is the most-watched long-term trend indicator in finance. Price above the 200-day is considered a bull market for that stock; below is considered a bear market. Many institutional investors use this line to define 'investable' vs 'not investable.'
entries.watch_for: Price can stay on the wrong side of the 200-day for long periods during corrections or recoveries. Use it as a regime indicator, not a daily signal. 'Golden cross' (50-day crosses above 200-day) and 'death cross' (50-day crosses below 200-day) are watched but not automatically reliable.

entries.name: 52-Week Range Percentile
entries.what: Where the current price sits within the stock's 52-week high-low range, expressed as a percentile (0-100).
entries.how: Percentile = (Current Price − 52w Low) / (52w High − 52w Low) × 100. At the 52w low it's 0%; at the 52w high it's 100%; at the midpoint it's 50%.
entries.why: Range percentile gives instant context for whether you're buying near the highs or the lows. A high-quality company at the 20th percentile of its range may be an opportunity; at the 95th percentile, you're paying top-of-range prices and need the fundamentals to justify it.
entries.watch_for: Range percentile is relative to ONE year of history. A stock at the 90th percentile of its 52w range may actually be near multi-year lows after a long decline. Combine with multi-year price context for full perspective.

entries.name: Suggested Entry Price
entries.what: The price at which the technical model recommends entering a position based on current setup and indicators.
entries.how: Derived from support levels, moving average proximity, recent consolidation zones, and risk/reward optimization. Usually near current price if technicals are favorable, or below current price if waiting for a pullback.
entries.why: Removes the emotional 'should I buy now or wait?' question by giving you a specific price. If you believe the fundamentals and the technicals support the signal, the suggested entry is where the model thinks the reward-to-risk is most favorable.
entries.watch_for: Suggested entries are snapshots — they may become stale quickly as the stock moves. A missed entry isn't necessarily a reason to chase; wait for the next setup or adjust your entry on new information.

entries.name: Suggested Stop Loss
entries.what: The price at which to exit the position if the thesis breaks down, representing the maximum loss you're willing to accept.
entries.how: Typically placed below a key support level (recent low, moving average, volatility-adjusted distance from entry). Often 8-15% below entry price.
entries.why: Stop losses protect capital from catastrophic single-trade losses. A disciplined 10% stop means a 50% portfolio drawdown requires seven consecutive max-losses in a row — extremely unlikely with good position sizing.
entries.watch_for: Stops can be hit by short-term noise even when the long-term thesis is intact. Mental stops (re-evaluate at $X) vs hard stops (automatic sell at $X) are different strategies — mental stops preserve your decision-making, hard stops preserve your discipline. Pick deliberately.

entries.name: Target Price
entries.what: The price at which the analysis suggests the stock's upside is largely captured and the position should be trimmed or exited.
entries.how: Typically derived from fair value models (DCF, EPV), peer multiples applied to forward earnings, or technical resistance levels.
entries.why: Targets give you a plan for WHEN to take profits. Without a target, you risk riding winners up and then all the way back down. A pre-committed target turns a speculative position into a planned trade with defined exit conditions.
entries.watch_for: Targets can be too conservative (forcing you out of compounders too early) or too aggressive (giving back gains waiting for unrealistic prices). Good targets reflect both valuation and the probability of reaching them.

entries.name: Risk/Reward Ratio
entries.what: The ratio of potential profit to potential loss for a given trade setup, comparing the distance from entry to target against the distance from entry to stop.
entries.how: R/R = (Target − Entry) / (Entry − Stop). A 3:1 R/R means the potential gain is 3x the potential loss.
entries.why: Position sizing and trade selection both depend on R/R. Even a trade with only 40% win probability can be profitable if R/R is 3:1 or higher. Trades with poor R/R (below 2:1) need very high win rates to be worthwhile.
entries.watch_for: R/R assumes you actually hit the target or stop — it doesn't account for the probability of each. A 10:1 R/R setup that only hits target 5% of the time is worse than a 2:1 setup that hits 60% of the time. Combine R/R with conviction/probability.

categories.title: Business Profile
categories.description: Qualitative assessments of the company's competitive position and durability.

entries.name: Moat Strength
entries.what: An assessment of how defensible the company's competitive advantage is against rivals. Rated as NONE, NARROW, MODERATE, or WIDE.
entries.how: Derived from factors like ROIC stability, pricing power, switching costs, network effects, brand strength, scale advantages, and patent protection. Higher ROIC sustained over years is the strongest indicator of a wide moat.
entries.why: Warren Buffett's defining investment question: 'Can competitors take market share or pricing power away from this business?' A wide moat means the company can compound value for years without being disrupted. A narrow or no moat means today's high returns won't last.
entries.watch_for: Moats erode. Newspapers, cable TV, and brick-and-mortar retail all had wide moats that evaporated. Moat analysis is backward-looking — always ask 'what could make this moat go away?' and weight the probability of disruption.

entries.name: Market Position (LEADER/CHALLENGER/NICHE)
entries.what: A classification of the company's role in its industry. LEADER is #1 or #2 by share, CHALLENGER is gaining on leaders, NICHE serves a specific segment without competing broadly.
entries.how: Based on revenue share, growth rate vs industry, and competitive dynamics. Leaders have scale and pricing power; challengers are investing to win share; niche players compete on specialization.
entries.why: Market position shapes strategy. Leaders focus on defending share; challengers focus on taking share; niche players focus on depth over breadth. Understanding which strategy the company is running tells you what to expect from its capital allocation and growth profile.
entries.watch_for: Being a 'challenger' isn't inherently better than being a 'leader' — it depends on whether the challenge is working. A perpetual challenger that never catches the leader is just wasting capital.

categories.title: Raw Financial Line Items
categories.description: The core financial statement items that underlie all the higher-level metrics.

entries.name: Revenue (Sales)
entries.what: The total amount of money generated from sales of goods and services before any expenses are subtracted. Also called 'the top line.'
entries.how: Sum of all sales transactions during the period, after discounts and returns but before any costs.
entries.why: Revenue is the most fundamental measure of a company's size and growth. It's also the hardest line to fake — while profits can be engineered, revenue must ultimately come from real customers paying real money.
entries.watch_for: Be suspicious of rapid revenue growth that doesn't flow through to profits or cash flow. Also watch for revenue recognition games — companies can pull forward revenue through aggressive booking or delay it through conservative recognition.

entries.name: Cost of Revenue (COGS)
entries.what: The direct costs of producing the goods or services sold during the period — raw materials, direct labor, and direct overhead.
entries.how: Sum of all direct production costs. For software, this is mostly hosting and support; for manufacturing, it's raw materials, labor, and factory overhead.
entries.why: Tracking COGS relative to revenue reveals whether the business is getting more or less efficient at its core production. Rising COGS as a percentage of revenue is a warning sign of input cost pressure or loss of pricing power.
entries.watch_for: Different industries include different things in COGS. Software companies often have very low COGS but high 'operating expenses' below the gross profit line. Don't compare COGS across industries.

entries.name: Gross Profit
entries.what: Revenue minus cost of revenue. The amount of money left after paying the direct costs of producing what was sold.
entries.how: Gross Profit = Revenue − Cost of Revenue. Expressed in dollars (as a line item) or as a percentage of revenue (gross margin).
entries.why: Gross profit funds all other activities — R&D, sales, marketing, general overhead, and ultimately shareholder returns. Companies with strong gross profit have room to invest in growth and weather downturns; companies with weak gross profit are always one bad quarter from crisis.
entries.watch_for: Gross profit alone isn't actionable — always look at the gross margin percentage to see whether absolute gross profit is improving because the business is better or just because revenue is growing.

entries.name: Operating Expenses (OpEx)
entries.what: The costs of running the business that aren't directly tied to producing the product — sales, marketing, R&D, general administration.
entries.how: Includes SG&A (Selling, General & Administrative), R&D (Research & Development), and other operating costs. Excludes COGS, interest, and taxes.
entries.why: OpEx tells you how much the company spends on growth, infrastructure, and overhead. Growing companies often have OpEx growing faster than revenue (investing in the future); mature companies should have OpEx growing slower than revenue (operating leverage).
entries.watch_for: SG&A growing faster than revenue in a mature business suggests sloppy cost control. R&D cuts in struggling businesses often foreshadow competitive decline — short-term profit boost at the cost of long-term position.

entries.name: Operating Income (EBIT)
entries.what: Earnings from the core business operations, before subtracting interest and taxes. Also called EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes) or Operating Profit.
entries.how: Operating Income = Revenue − COGS − Operating Expenses. Excludes non-operating items like interest expense, investment gains, and tax.
entries.why: Operating income is the best measure of how profitable the core business is, independent of capital structure (debt levels) and tax jurisdiction. It's what most valuation models (DCF, EVA, EPV) use as their starting point.
entries.watch_for: Operating income can be distorted by one-time items like restructuring charges, asset sales, or impairments. Always check for non-recurring items before treating operating income as the 'normalized' level.

entries.name: Net Income (Net Profit)
entries.what: The company's actual bottom-line profit after all expenses, interest, taxes, and one-time items. The amount that legally belongs to shareholders.
entries.how: Net Income = Operating Income − Interest Expense − Taxes ± One-Time Items. The very last line on the income statement.
entries.why: Net income is the official profit number reported to shareholders and used for EPS calculations. It's the starting point for most retail investors' analysis and the basis for P/E ratios.
entries.watch_for: Net income is the most manipulable line on the income statement. It includes discretionary items like asset writedowns, tax strategies, and one-time gains. For a truer picture of business economics, always cross-check against operating cash flow and free cash flow.

entries.name: Free Cash Flow (FCF)
entries.what: The cash the company generates from operations after accounting for the capital expenditures needed to maintain and grow the business. The real cash available for dividends, buybacks, debt reduction, or reinvestment.
entries.how: FCF = Cash from Operations − Capital Expenditures. Some calculations also subtract required working capital investment.
entries.why: FCF is the most important single number in fundamental analysis. Unlike accounting earnings, it's very hard to manipulate. All valuation ultimately traces back to future free cash flows — dividends come from FCF, buybacks come from FCF, debt reduction comes from FCF.
entries.watch_for: One-time capex swings (big expansion projects, cyclical investments) can make single-year FCF misleading. Use 3-5 year averages for a more reliable signal. Also distinguish between 'maintenance capex' (required to keep running) and 'growth capex' (invested for expansion).

entries.name: Cash from Operations (OCF)
entries.what: The cash generated by the core business activities during the period, before any investments in property, equipment, or acquisitions.
entries.how: Starts with net income and adjusts for non-cash items (depreciation, stock-based comp), changes in working capital (receivables, payables, inventory), and other non-cash adjustments.
entries.why: Operating cash flow reveals whether the business is actually generating cash or just reporting profits on paper. A company with strong net income but weak operating cash flow has a problem — usually working capital that's ballooning or aggressive accounting.
entries.watch_for: The gap between net income and operating cash flow is one of the best fraud-detection tools in accounting. Enron famously had strong reported profits but terrible operating cash flow for years before the collapse.

entries.name: Total Assets
entries.what: The total value of everything the company owns — cash, receivables, inventory, property, equipment, intangibles, and investments.
entries.how: Sum of current assets and non-current assets from the balance sheet. Represents the company's total resource base.
entries.why: Total assets is the denominator for efficiency ratios like ROA (Return on Assets) and asset turnover. A company that generates $2 of revenue per $1 of assets is twice as capital-efficient as one generating $1 per $1.
entries.watch_for: Goodwill from acquisitions inflates total assets without adding productive capacity. Companies with large goodwill balances should be analyzed with and without goodwill to see the underlying operating business.

entries.name: Total Debt
entries.what: The sum of all interest-bearing debt the company owes — short-term borrowings, long-term debt, bonds, notes payable, and capital leases.
entries.how: Short-term debt + long-term debt + current portion of long-term debt. Usually reported on the balance sheet as separate line items that need to be summed.
entries.why: Total debt drives leverage ratios, interest expense, and financial fragility. High debt amplifies returns in good times but destroys companies in bad times.
entries.watch_for: Operating leases now appear on balance sheets as debt (after 2019 accounting changes). Some companies also have 'off-balance-sheet' obligations that function like debt — purchase commitments, guarantees, take-or-pay contracts. Read the footnotes.

entries.name: Total Equity (Shareholders' Equity)
entries.what: The residual value of the company that belongs to shareholders after subtracting all liabilities from all assets. Also called 'book value.'
entries.how: Total Equity = Total Assets − Total Liabilities. Includes paid-in capital, retained earnings, and accumulated other comprehensive income.
entries.why: Book value is the accounting representation of what shareholders own. Price-to-book (P/B) compares the market's valuation to this accounting value. ROE measures profits relative to this equity base.
entries.watch_for: Book value understates intangible assets (brand, customer relationships, software) that don't appear on the balance sheet. Companies with strong intangibles (brands, platforms) often trade far above book value for good reasons. Book value is most meaningful for asset-heavy businesses like banks and real estate.

entries.name: Cash and Equivalents
entries.what: The company's liquid cash holdings — checking accounts, money market funds, short-term treasury bills, and other investments that can be converted to cash in 90 days or less.
entries.how: Reported directly on the balance sheet as 'Cash and cash equivalents,' sometimes combined with 'short-term investments' or 'marketable securities.'
entries.why: Cash is optionality. A cash-rich company can survive downturns, take advantage of distressed acquisitions, fund buybacks opportunistically, and avoid forced selling. Cash is also insurance against things going wrong unexpectedly.
entries.watch_for: Large cash balances in offshore jurisdictions may be trapped by tax consequences of repatriation. Very large cash hoards relative to business size often indicate management lacks good reinvestment opportunities — not always a bad sign but worth understanding.

entries.name: EBITDA
entries.what: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. A measure of operating profitability that strips out both capital structure effects (interest) and accounting effects (depreciation).
entries.how: EBITDA = Operating Income + Depreciation + Amortization. Or equivalently: Net Income + Interest + Taxes + Depreciation + Amortization.
entries.why: EBITDA allows comparison of operating profitability across companies with different debt levels, tax situations, and capital intensities. It's widely used in M&A because it approximates the cash flow available to a buyer before capital structure decisions.
entries.watch_for: Depreciation is NOT a fake expense — it represents the real wear-and-tear of the asset base. EBITDA ignores this reality, which is why it can overstate profitability in asset-heavy businesses. Charlie Munger famously called EBITDA 'bullshit earnings.' Always cross-check with FCF.

entries.name: Shares Outstanding
entries.what: The total number of shares of stock that the company has issued and are currently held by investors. Includes all share classes.
entries.how: Reported on the income statement (for EPS calculation) and balance sheet. 'Basic' shares is actual outstanding; 'diluted' includes potential shares from options, convertibles, and RSUs.
entries.why: Share count directly determines per-share values. EPS, dividend per share, and fair value per share all depend on this number. Changes in share count (buybacks vs dilution) are one of the most important forms of capital return (or capital extraction).
entries.watch_for: Always use diluted shares for valuation and per-share calculations. Basic shares understate future dilution from stock-based compensation, which can be significant in tech companies where SBC runs 10-20% of revenue annually.
